title: [FIRRTL] CHIRRTL MPort Redesign
body:
CHIRRTL memories are one of the more problematic pieces of FIRRTL and their design and implementation has some well known issues.  This issue does not try to redesign the way CHIRRTL memories work and create a new Chisel API to support it, this issue is about capturing how behavioral memories currently work, and how we can implement them in CIRCT so that we can be a drop in replacement SFC.  For those interested, some work towards a CHIRRTL memory redesign can be found here: https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl/pull/1821

---

In Chisel, the synchronous read memory API is deliberately creating IR which violates the spec, specifically that an `mport` is being used outside of the scope it is defined in:
```
circuit Bar :
  module Bar :
    input clock: Clock
    input addr: UInt<3>
    input cond: UInt<1>
    output out: UInt<1>

    smem mem : UInt<1>[8]

    when cond:
      infer mport r = mem[addr], clock
    ; `r` is used outside the of the when block it is declared in.
    out <= r
```

One attempt to fix this problem involved creating a wire operation in the outer scope:

```
  ; Create the wire `foo` in the outer scope, and replace all uses of `r` with the wire
  wire foo : UInt<1>
  when cond:
    infer mport r = mem[addr], clock
    foo <= r
  out <= foo
```

This approach has some flaws, for example you can no longer write a value to the memory port:

```
  wire foo : UInt<1>
  when cond:
    infer mport r = mem[addr], clock
    foo <= r
  ; This is now the last connect to the wire `foo`, and the port will not be inferred as a ReadWrite port
  foo <= in
```
 For more discussion of this approach, see issue https://github.com/chipsalliance/chisel3/pull/2064 and https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl/pull/2328.
 
 ---
 
 Since we have found it hard to fix this problem at its source, we have decided that we need to support this FIRRTL.  We need to:
 1. Make sure that a port can be used outside of the declaration location
 2. The port declaration location is captured for enable inference
 3. Memory port direction (read/write) inference still works
 
 Our idea is to split the creation of an `mport` into two operations:
 1. `firrtl.memoryPort.def`
 This operation represents the creation of a memory port.  This operation returns two values, the `data` port of the memory, and an `mport` declaration which can be used to set the the enable. The data result of this operation can be used to read or write to the memory.  This operation should be emitted into the module scope, which allows simulating the ability to use a memory outside of the scope it is declared.

2. `firrtl.memoryport.access`
 This operation enables the memory.  This operation should be emitted into the location of the original mport declaration.  This operation also handles the connection of a clock to the memory port.

 ```diff
 diff --git a/mport-madness/Foo.mlir b/mport-madness/Foo2.mlir
index 8b31643e..f20fd172 100644
--- a/mport-madness/Foo.mlir
+++ b/mport-madness/Foo2.mlir
@@ -1,8 +1,8 @@
 module  {
   firrtl.circuit "Foo"   {
     firrtl.module @Foo(in %clock: !firrtl.clock, in %reset: !firrtl.uint<1>, in %enable: !firrtl.uint<1>, in %addr: !firrtl.uint<1>, out %out: !firrtl.uint<1>) {
       %mem = firrtl.smem Undefined  {name = "mem"} : !firrtl.vector<uint<1>, 2>
+      %foo_data, %foo_port = firrtl.memoryPort.def Read %mem : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.memport
       %en = firrtl.wire  : !firrtl.uint<1>
       %_foo_WIRE = firrtl.wire  : !firrtl.uint
       %invalid_ui = firrtl.invalidvalue : !firrtl.uint
@@ -14,15 +14,14 @@ module  {
         %_foo_T = firrtl.node %2  : !firrtl.uint
         %3 = firrtl.bits %_foo_T 0 to 0 : (!firrtl.uint) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
         %_foo_T_1 = firrtl.node %3  : !firrtl.uint<1>
-        %foo = firrtl.memoryport Read %mem, %_foo_T_1, %clock  {name = "foo"} : (!firrtl.vector<uint<1>, 2>, !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.clock) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
+        firrtl.memoryport.access %foo_port, %_foo_T_1, %clock : !firrtl.mport, !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
         firrtl.skip
       }
       %c1_ui1 = firrtl.constant 1 : !firrtl.uint<1>
-      %1 = firrtl.eq %foo, %c1_ui1 : (!firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
+      %1 = firrtl.eq %foo_data, %c1_ui1 : (!firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
       %_en_T = firrtl.node %1  : !firrtl.uint<1>
       firrtl.connect %en, %_en_T : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
-      firrtl.connect %out, %foo : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+      firrtl.connect %out, %foo_data : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
     }
   }
 }
 ```
Some comments:
- This is a pretty direct mapping of the problem to a solution, nothing clever going on here
- Since the `firrtl.access` enable is not using connect semantics, this needs to be resolved before ExpandWhens

 ---
 
 An alternative approach is to follow some of the design here: https://github.com/chipsalliance/firrtl/issues/727#issuecomment-547102869

In this scheme, we add a `address`, `enable`, and `clock` results to the memory port.  The memory port should be created at the module scope, and the `data` value can be used outside of the scope of the original declaration.  The enable should be set to 1 in the scope of the original memport declaration.
   
 ```diff
 diff --git a/mport-madness/Foo.mlir b/mport-madness/Foo2.mlir
index 8b31643e..f20fd172 100644
--- a/mport-madness/Foo.mlir
+++ b/mport-madness/Foo2.mlir
@@ -1,8 +1,8 @@
 module  {
   firrtl.circuit "Foo"   {
     firrtl.module @Foo(in %clock: !firrtl.clock, in %reset: !firrtl.uint<1>, in %enable: !firrtl.uint<1>, in %addr: !firrtl.uint<1>, out %out: !firrtl.uint<1>) {
       %mem = firrtl.smem Undefined  {name = "mem"} : !firrtl.vector<uint<1>, 2>
+      %foo_data, %foo_address, %foo_enable, %foo_clock = firrtl.memoryport Read %mem : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>,  !firrtl.clock
+      !firrtl.connect %foo_enable, %c0_ui1 : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+      !firrtl.connect %foo_address, %invalid_ui1 : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+      !firrtl.connect %foo_clock, %invalid_ui1 : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
       %en = firrtl.wire  : !firrtl.uint<1>
       %_foo_WIRE = firrtl.wire  : !firrtl.uint
       %invalid_ui = firrtl.invalidvalue : !firrtl.uint
@@ -14,15 +14,14 @@ module  {
         %_foo_T = firrtl.node %2  : !firrtl.uint
         %3 = firrtl.bits %_foo_T 0 to 0 : (!firrtl.uint) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
         %_foo_T_1 = firrtl.node %3  : !firrtl.uint<1>
-        %foo = firrtl.memoryport Read %mem, %_foo_T_1, %clock  {name = "foo"} : (!firrtl.vector<uint<1>, 2>, !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.clock) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
+        firrtl.connect %foo_address, %_foo_T_1 : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+        firrtl.connect %foo_enable, %c1_ui1 : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+        firtrl.connect %foo_clock, %clock: !firrtl.clock, !firrtl.clock
         firrtl.skip
       }
       %c1_ui1 = firrtl.constant 1 : !firrtl.uint<1>
-      %1 = firrtl.eq %foo, %c1_ui1 : (!firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
+      %1 = firrtl.eq %foo_data, %c1_ui1 : (!firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>) -> !firrtl.uint<1>
       %_en_T = firrtl.node %1  : !firrtl.uint<1>
       firrtl.connect %en, %_en_T : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
-      firrtl.connect %out, %foo : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
+      firrtl.connect %out, %foo_data : !firrtl.uint<1>, !firrtl.uint<1>
     }
   }
 }
 ```
 
 Some comments:
 - Another way of looking at this approach is that it moves part of RemoveCHIRRTL into the FIRParser
- if this is a sync-read memory, then we will have to throw away all the connections to the enable port and infer new ones
- this has limited round-tripability with new FIREmitter
